items.title: Primary chancre
items.contentHtml:
Primary syphilis

  • Painless hard chancre appears after 9-90 days of incubation; It is a clean ulcer, with indurated edges and a clean, non-painful base.
  • Most common location: genital, but can be oral or anal.
  • Key differential diagnosis: genital herpes (painful, vesicles), chancroid (painful, irregular edges), lymphogranuloma venereum.
  • Serology (VDRL) may be negative in the first 2-4 weeks; dark field testing of exudate is the initial diagnostic standard.
  • The chancre resolves spontaneously in 3-6 weeks, but the infection persists without treatment.

items.foco: Secondary syphilis: palmoplantar rash and condylomas
items.title: Rash and condylomas
items.contentHtml:
Secondary syphilis

  • Appears 4-10 weeks after the chancre; classic manifestation: palmoplantar rash maculo-papular, non-pruritic, affecting palms and soles.
  • Condylomas flata (or condylomas lata): moist, highly contagious papular lesions in folds and perianal area.
  • It may be accompanied by fever, generalized lymphadenopathy, patchy alopecia and mucosal lesions (plaques). mucous membranes).
  • Differential diagnosis: palmoplantar psoriasis, pityriasis rosea, reaction to drugs; Serology (VDRL + FTA-ABS) is almost 100% positive.
  • Without treatment, it progresses to latency or tertiary.

items.title: Late manifestations
items.contentHtml:
Tertiary syphilis

  • Occurs years after the initial infection (3-30 years).
  • Gumas: destructive granulomatous lesions in the skin, bone or viscera; rare today due to antibiotics.
  • Late neurosyphilis: tabes dorsalis (loss of proprioception, positive Romberg sign) and progressive general paralysis (dementia, Argyll Robertson pupils).
  • Cardiovascular: syphilitic aortitis with aortic insufficiency and ascending aortic aneurysm.
  • Serology (VDRL) may be negative in late neurosyphilis; FTA-ABS is usually positive.

items.foco: VDRL test: screening and activity monitoring
items.title: VDRL in screening
items.contentHtml:
VDRL

  • VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Laboratory) is a non-treponemal test that detects anticardiolipin antibodies; it is used for screening and activity monitoring.
  • Results are reported in titles (for example 1:16); a 4-fold decrease (2 dilutions) at 6-12 months post-treatment indicates adequate response.
  • False positives: pregnancy, lupus, human immunodeficiency virus, acute viral infections; It is confirmed with FTA-ABS.
  • In primary syphilis it may be initially negative; repeat after 4 weeks if high blood pressure is suspected.

items.title: Confirmatory FTA-ABS
items.contentHtml:
FTA-ABS

  • FTA-ABS (Fluorescent Treponemal Antibody Absorption) is a specific treponemal test for confirmation of infection.
  • Detects antibodies against Treponema pallidum; remains positive for life, even after successful treatment.
  • Not useful for activity monitoring; only to confirm if VDRL/RPR is positive.
  • There may be false positives in other treponematoses (yaws, bejel) and in autoimmune diseases (rare).

items.foco: Neurosyphilis: lumbar puncture with VDRL in cerebrospinal fluid
items.title: Diagnosis of neurosyphilis
items.contentHtml:
Neurosyphilis

  • Indication for lumbar puncture with VDRL in cerebrospinal fluid: late latent syphilis or syphilis of unknown duration with neurological symptoms, or human immunodeficiency virus coinfection with serum titers ≥1:32.
  • Diagnostic criteria: positive VDRL in cerebrospinal fluid (specific but not sensitive); if negative and highly suspected, perform FTA-ABS on cerebrospinal fluid (high sensitivity).
  • Lymphocytic pleocytosis and proteinorrachia support the diagnosis.
  • Treatment: crystalline penicillin G intravenous 18-24 million IU/day for 10-14 days.

items.foco: Congenital syphilis: RPR titers in the newborn
items.title: congenital syphilis
items.contentHtml:
Congenital syphilis

  • Evaluate all newborns born to mothers with untreated or treated gestational syphilis <4 weeks before delivery.
  • Compare RPR titers in the blood of the newborn with those of the mother; if the neonatal titer is ≥4 times higher than the maternal one, there is congenital infection.
  • Clinical findings: syphilitic rhinitis (bloody nasal discharge), vesicular skin lesions (syphilitic pemphigus), osteochondritis, hepatosplenomegaly.
  • Treatment: crystalline penicillin G intravenous 50,000 U/kg each 12 hours (first week) or procaine penicillin G intramuscular 50,000 U/kg/day for 10 days.

items.foco: Benzathine penicillin G: first line in syphilis
items.title: Benzathine Penicillin G
items.contentHtml:
Benzathine Penicillin G

  • Benzathine Penicillin G is the first-line treatment for primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis.
  • Dose: 2.4 million IU intramuscular as a single dose (for early syphilis); for late latent or unknown duration: 2.4 million IU intramuscular every week for 3 doses (total 7.2 million IU).
  • In pregnant women: same regimen; if allergic to penicillin, desensitize and administer penicillin.
  • Do not use doxycycline during pregnancy; in non-pregnant women with allergies: doxycycline 100 mg PO every 12 hours for 14 days.

items.title: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
items.contentHtml:
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

  • Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: fever, chills, myalgia and headache that occurs 2-24 hours after the first dose of penicillin.
  • More common in early syphilis (primary and secondary) and in pregnant women (can trigger uterine contractions).
  • Mechanism: massive release of endotoxins due to lysis of spirochetes.
  • It is not an allergic reaction; It is managed with antipyretics (paracetamol) and does not contraindicate continuing treatment.
  • In neurosyphilis, the reaction can exacerbate neurological symptoms; Premedication with corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg/day for 3 days) is recommended in some protocols.

items.foco: Latent syphilis: positive serology without clinical symptoms
items.title: latent syphilis
items.contentHtml:
Latent syphilis

  • Definition: positive serology (VDRL/RPR and FTA-ABS) without clinical signs or symptoms of syphilis.
  • Classification: early latent (<1 year of infection) and late latent (≥1 year or unknown duration).
  • Differentiation is key for treatment: early receives a single dose of benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million IU intramuscular; late receives 3 doses weekly.
  • History of previous treatment, serological titers and sexual contact help determine the stage.

items.foco: Coinfection with human immunodeficiency virus: increased risk of neurosyphilis
items.title: human immunodeficiency virus and syphilis
items.contentHtml:
human immunodeficiency virus co-infection

  • human immunodeficiency virus positive patients have increased risk of neurosyphilis and accelerated progression of the disease.
  • Indications for lumbar puncture in human immunodeficiency virus: late latent syphilis, serum titers ≥1:32, or neurological symptoms regardless of stage.
  • Treatment: same guidelines as in human immunodeficiency virus negative; However, some experts recommend 3 doses of benzathine penicillin G for early syphilis in human immunodeficiency virus with high titers.
  • Follow-up: serological control at 3, 6, 9, 12 and 24 months; If there is no 4-fold decrease in titers, consider retreatment.

items.foco: Neurosyphilis Treatment: intravenous Crystalline Penicillin G
items.title: Neurosyphilis: intravenous treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of neurosyphilis

  • Crystalline penicillin G intravenous is the treatment of choice: 18-24 million IU/day in divided doses every 4 hours (or continuous infusion) for 10-14 days.
  • Alternative if intravenous access is not possible: procaine penicillin G intramuscular 2.4 million IU/day plus probenecid 500 mg PO every 6 hours for 10-14 days.
  • After completing the intravenous regimen, some recommend 3 weekly doses of benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million IU intramuscular to cover possible systemic infection.
  • Control: repeat lumbar puncture after 6 months; A decrease in pleocytosis and normalization of VDRL in cerebrospinal fluid is expected.
